article.title: Artemis II Lifts Off From Kennedy Space Center, Sending Four Astronauts Toward the Moon for the First Time in 53 Years
article.summary: NASA's SLS rocket launches the Orion spacecraft on a 10-day lunar flyby mission, the first crewed voyage beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Artemis II mission launched from Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39B on April 1, 2026, sending four astronauts toward the Moon aboard the Orion spacecraft, according to [NASA's live launch updates](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/). The 10-day mission marks the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

The launch follows [prior reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at Kennedy Space Center and the start of the countdown sequence.

## What We Know

The Space Launch System rocket, standing 322 feet tall and generating over 8.8 million pounds of thrust, lifted off during a two-hour launch window that opened at 6:24 p.m. EDT, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Preparations included the loading of 700,000 gallons of liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen into the SLS core stage, as detailed in [NASA's launch day blog](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/).

The four-person crew consists of Commander Reid Wiseman, a former U.S. Navy fighter pilot; Pilot Victor Glover, the first person of color to travel beyond low Earth orbit; Mission Specialist Christina Koch, who holds NASA's record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days; and Canadian Space Agency Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen, the first non-American to fly beyond Earth orbit, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). The crew named their Orion spacecraft "Integrity," as reported by [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-nasa-moon-launch-time-astronauts-how-watch-what-know-rcna255627).

The mission will cover approximately 685,000 miles over 10 days on a free-return trajectory around the Moon, as reported by [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). The crew is expected to approach within approximately 6,000 miles of the lunar surface and could surpass the Apollo 13 distance record of 248,655 miles from Earth, set in 1970, according to [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-nasa-moon-launch-time-astronauts-how-watch-what-know-rcna255627). The reentry is expected to exceed 25,000 mph, setting a record for the fastest crewed spacecraft return, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

Splashdown is projected for approximately April 10 in the Pacific Ocean off San Diego, according to [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-nasa-moon-launch-time-astronauts-how-watch-what-know-rcna255627).

## What We Don't Know

While the launch has occurred, the mission's most critical phases lie ahead. The trans-lunar injection burn, which will send Orion on its trajectory toward the Moon, is scheduled for approximately two days after launch. The proximity operations demonstration, during which the crew will manually test Orion's thrusters at roughly 3.5 hours into the mission, will be the first real-time test of the spacecraft's crewed maneuvering capabilities, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

The mission does not include a lunar landing. That milestone belongs to Artemis III, which [NASA has previously indicated](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) is now targeted for 2028.

## Significance

Artemis II represents the first crewed flight of the SLS rocket and the Orion spacecraft, as noted by [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii/). The agency has described the SLS as the only rocket capable of sending Orion, astronauts, and cargo directly to the Moon in a single launch. The mission builds on the uncrewed Artemis I flight in 2022, which tested the SLS and Orion systems over a 25-day mission.

The launch also carries geopolitical significance. Jeremy Hansen's presence makes Canada the second nation to send an astronaut beyond low Earth orbit, a reflection of the Canadian Space Agency's contributions to the Artemis program, including the Canadarm3 robotic arm planned for the Lunar Gateway station.

Commander Reid Wiseman captured the moment: "I think the nation and the world has been waiting a long time to do this again," as reported by [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-nasa-moon-launch-time-astronauts-how-watch-what-know-rcna255627).
